# Git Configuration Reference

<!-- Auto-generated by git_doctor.py — do not edit by hand. -->

| **Generated by**    | `git_doctor.py` v3.0.0                                 |
| :------------------ | :----------------------------------------------------- |
| **Timestamp**       | 2026-03-16 20:34:33 UTC                                |
| **Catalog updated** | 2026-03-16 (when config entries were last reviewed)    |

| **Regenerate**      | Run the command above to refresh with current settings |
| **Total keys**      | 62 commonly used keys across 17 sections               |

## How This File Works

This file is a generated reference of commonly used git config keys.
Each entry has a table with **Current value**, **Desired value**,
**Current scope**, **Desired scope**, **Valid values**, and **Valid scopes**
columns. To change a setting:

1. Set **both** `Desired value` **and** `Desired scope` in the entry's table.
2. Use `--dry-run` to preview what would change:
   `python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-from git-config-reference.md --dry-run`
3. Apply your changes:
   `python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-from git-config-reference.md`

> **Important:** Both columns must be set for an entry to be applied.
> If only one is filled in, the entry is skipped with an error.
> Complete entries in other sections are still applied.

### Applying All Recommended Values at Once

If you want to set every config key to the catalog's recommended
value and scope (overwriting any existing values):

```bash
python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-recommended --dry-run   # preview
python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-recommended              # apply
```

> **Warning:** `--apply-recommended` overwrites existing values.
> It resets your config to the catalog baseline. For per-key
> control, edit this file and use `--apply-from` instead.

### Applying Only the Core Minimal Set

If you want a safer, lighter-touch setup with just 12 high-impact
keys (e.g. `pull.rebase`, `fetch.prune`, `rerere.enabled`):

```bash
python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-recommended-minimal --dry-run   # preview
python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-recommended-minimal              # apply
```

> **Tip:** `--apply-recommended-minimal` is ideal for first-time setup.
> Use `--apply-recommended` later to adopt the full catalog baseline.

---

> **Note:** This reference covers commonly used git configuration
> options — it is not an exhaustive list. Git has 500+ configuration
> keys; this file curates the ones most relevant to day-to-day
> development workflows. See the [Glossary](#glossary) at the end
> for definitions of common git config terminology, and
> [Customizing This Reference](#customizing-this-reference) for how
> to add or remove config entries.

## Scope Guide

| Scope      | File Location                                                                    | Applies To            |
| :--------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :-------------------- |
| **local**  | `.git/config` in the repository                                                  | This repository only  |
| **global** | `~/.gitconfig` or `~/.config/git/config`                                         | All your repositories |
| **system** | `/etc/gitconfig` (Linux/macOS) or `C:\Program Files\Git\etc\gitconfig` (Windows) | All users on machine  |

**Precedence:** local > global > system.

Set most configs at **global** scope so they apply everywhere,
then override at **local** scope for project-specific needs
(e.g. different email for work repos, project-specific commit template).

> **How `--apply-from` works:** When you edit this file and run
> `--apply-from`, **both** the Desired value and Desired scope columns
> must be set for an entry to apply. If only one is filled in, the
> entry is skipped with an error message. Complete entries in other
> sections are still applied normally.

### Updating Config Values

```bash
# Read a value (shows effective value from highest-priority scope)
git config <key>

# Read with origin (shows which file defines the value)
git config --show-origin <key>

# Set at different scopes
git config --global <key> <value>    # ~/.gitconfig
git config --local <key> <value>     # .git/config (this repo only)
git config --system <key> <value>    # system-wide (requires admin)

# Remove a value
git config --global --unset <key>

# List all settings with their scopes
git config --list --show-origin
```

## Core

*Fundamental git behavior: line endings, editor, pager, file handling.*

### `core.autocrlf`

Line ending conversion: 'true'=CRLF<>LF (Windows), 'input'=LF on commit only, 'false'=off

> **What this does:** Windows and macOS/Linux use different invisible characters at the end of each line. This setting tells git how to convert between them so files look correct on every operating system.

| Property              | Value                                     |
| :-------------------- | :---------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `false`                                   |
| **Current scope**     | global                                    |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`, `input`                  |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`               |
| **Recommended value** | 'input' on macOS/Linux, 'true' on Windows |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                    |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                             |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                             |

### `core.editor`

Default text editor for commit messages, interactive rebase, and other editing

> **What this does:** When you run `git commit` without the `-m` flag, git opens a text editor so you can write your commit message. This setting picks which editor opens. `code --wait` means VS Code.

| Property              | Value                                                           |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `code --wait`                                                   |
| **Current scope**     | global                                                          |
| **Valid values**      | Any editor command — e.g. `code --wait`, `vim`, `nano`, `emacs` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                     |
| **Recommended value** | e.g. 'code --wait', 'vim', 'nano'                               |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                          |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                   |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                   |

### `core.pager`

Pager program for git output (log, diff). 'delta' gives syntax-highlighted diffs

> **What this does:** When git output is longer than your terminal, it scrolls through a "pager" program (like `less`). `delta` is a popular pager that adds syntax highlighting and side-by-side diffs.

| Property              | Value                                                       |
| :-------------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                       |
| **Valid values**      | Any pager command — e.g. `delta`, `less -FRX`, `cat`, `bat` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                 |
| **Recommended value** | 'delta' for better diffs, or 'less -FRX'                    |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                               |

### `core.excludesfile`

Path to a global gitignore file applied to ALL repositories (editor/OS ignores)

> **What this does:** A personal `.gitignore` that applies to every repo on your machine. Put OS junk (`.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`) and editor files (`.idea/`, `.vscode/`) here so they never get committed anywhere.

| Property              | Value                                                                 |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                             |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                                 |
| **Valid values**      | Absolute path to a global gitignore file — e.g. `~/.gitignore_global` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                           |
| **Recommended value** | '~/.gitignore_global'                                                 |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                                |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                         |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                         |

### `core.filemode`

Track file permission (executable bit) changes. Windows doesn't support Unix perms

> **What this does:** On Linux/macOS, files can be marked as "executable." Windows doesn't have this concept, so git may think every file's permissions changed. Setting this to `false` on Windows silences those false diffs.

| Property              | Value                                                   |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Current value**     | `false`                                                 |
| **Current scope**     | local                                                   |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                                         |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                             |
| **Recommended value** | 'false' on Windows if you see spurious permission diffs |
| **Recommended scope** | local                                                   |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                           |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                           |

### `core.longpaths`

Enable paths longer than 260 chars on Windows. Required for deep node_modules

> **What this does:** Windows traditionally limits file paths to 260 characters. Some projects (especially with `node_modules/`) exceed this. Setting this to `true` removes the limit.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                       |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`             |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' on Windows           |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

### `core.quotepath`

Quote non-ASCII characters in pathnames. Disable to see UTF-8 filenames correctly

> **What this does:** By default, git escapes non-English characters in file names, showing them as `\nnn` sequences. Set to `false` to see the actual characters.

| Property              | Value                                            |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                        |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                            |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                                  |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                      |
| **Recommended value** | 'false' to display non-ASCII filenames correctly |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                           |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                    |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                    |

### `core.hooksPath`

Custom directory for git hooks. Usually managed by pre-commit

> **What this does:** Git hooks are scripts that run automatically at certain points (e.g. before a commit). This setting points git to a custom hooks folder. If you use pre-commit, it manages hooks for you — leave this unset.

| Property              | Value                                                                |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                            |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                                |
| **Valid values**      | Path to hooks directory — e.g. `.husky`, `.githooks`, or leave unset |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                          |
| **Recommended value** | Leave unset when using pre-commit                                    |
| **Recommended scope** | local                                                                |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                        |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                        |

### `core.eol`

Line ending style in working directory: 'lf', 'crlf', or 'native'

> **What this does:** Controls which line-ending style git uses in your working files. `lf` is standard for cross-platform projects. For per-file control, use a `.gitattributes` file instead.

| Property              | Value                                                   |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                               |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                   |
| **Valid values**      | `lf`, `crlf`, `native`                                  |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                             |
| **Recommended value** | 'lf' for cross-platform projects, or use .gitattributes |
| **Recommended scope** | local                                                   |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                           |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                           |

### `core.safecrlf`

Abort/warn if line ending conversion would lose data

> **What this does:** A safety net: if converting line endings would change a file in a way that can't be reversed, git warns or blocks the operation.

| Property              | Value                                             |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------ |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                         |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                             |
| **Valid values**      | `true` (abort), `warn` (warn only), `false` (off) |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                       |
| **Recommended value** | 'warn' for safety without blocking                |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                            |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                     |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                     |

### `core.symlinks`

Whether working tree supports symbolic links (auto-detected on clone)

> **What this does:** Symbolic links are shortcuts that point to another file. Some operating systems (especially older Windows) don't support them. Git auto-detects this on clone.

| Property              | Value                                                      |
| :-------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `false`                                                    |
| **Current scope**     | local                                                      |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false` (auto-detected on clone)                   |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                |
| **Recommended value** | Auto-detected; 'false' if symlinks cause issues on Windows |
| **Recommended scope** | local                                                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                              |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                              |

### `core.whitespace`

Which whitespace problems git notices (trailing-space, space-before-tab, etc.)

> **What this does:** Tells git which whitespace issues to flag — like trailing spaces at the end of a line, or spaces before tabs. Helpful for keeping code clean.

| Property              | Value                                                                             |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                                         |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                                             |
| **Valid values**      | Comma-separated list — e.g. `trailing-space,space-before-tab,indent-with-non-tab` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                                       |
| **Recommended value** | Default is fine for most projects                                                 |
| **Recommended scope** | local                                                                             |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                                     |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                                     |

### `core.ignorecase`

Whether filenames are case-insensitive (auto-detected on clone). Affects 'git mv'

> **What this does:** On Windows and macOS, `File.txt` and `file.txt` are the same file. On Linux, they're different. Git auto-detects this, but it affects whether `git mv` can rename a file by just changing its case.

| Property              | Value                                                            |
| :-------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `true`                                                           |
| **Current scope**     | local                                                            |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false` (auto-detected on clone)                         |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                      |
| **Recommended value** | Auto-detected; usually 'true' on Windows/macOS, 'false' on Linux |
| **Recommended scope** | local                                                            |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                    |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                    |
