## Fetch

*How git retrieves objects and refs from remotes.*

### `fetch.prune`

Auto-remove stale remote-tracking branches on every fetch

> **What this does:** When a teammate deletes a branch on GitHub, your local git still remembers it. With `fetch.prune = true`, those stale references are automatically cleaned up every time you fetch.

| Property              | Value                                      |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `true`                                     |
| **Current scope**     | global                                     |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                            |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' -- avoids stale branch accumulation |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                     |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                              |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                              |

### `fetch.prunetags`

Auto-remove stale remote tags on fetch

> **What this does:** Same idea as `fetch.prune`, but for tags. Removes references to remote tags that no longer exist.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `true`                      |
| **Current scope**     | global                      |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`             |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'true'                      |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

### `fetch.fsckobjects`

Verify integrity of objects received during fetch (catches corruption)

> **What this does:** Runs an integrity check on every object received from a remote. Catches corrupted data before it enters your repo. Slightly slower but much safer.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                       |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`             |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' for security         |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

---

## Pull

*Behavior when pulling changes from a remote branch.*

### `pull.rebase`

Rebase local commits on top of upstream when pulling. Gives linear history

> **What this does:** When you `git pull`, your local commits can either be "merged" on top (creating a merge commit) or "rebased" (replayed one by one on top of the remote changes). Rebase keeps the history cleaner and linear — no unnecessary merge commits cluttering your log.

| Property              | Value                                    |
| :-------------------- | :--------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `true`                                   |
| **Current scope**     | global                                   |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`, `merges`, `interactive` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`              |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' for linear history                |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                   |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                            |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                            |

### `pull.ff`

Fast-forward behavior on pull: 'only' refuses merge commits, 'true' allows any merge

> **What this does:** A "fast-forward" is when git can just move the branch pointer forward without creating a merge commit. Setting this to `only` means git refuses to pull if a fast-forward isn't possible, so you never get surprise merge commits.

| Property              | Value                                      |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                  |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                      |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`, `only`                    |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                |
| **Recommended value** | 'only' to prevent accidental merge commits |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                     |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                              |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                              |

---

## Push

*Defaults for pushing commits to remotes.*

### `push.default`

What to push when no refspec given: 'current'=same-named branch, 'simple'=default

> **What this does:** When you type `git push` without specifying a branch, git needs to decide what to push. `current` pushes your current branch to a remote branch with the same name. `simple` does the same but only if the upstream branch also has the same name.

| Property              | Value                                                  |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `current`                                              |
| **Current scope**     | global                                                 |
| **Valid values**      | `current`, `simple`, `upstream`, `matching`, `nothing` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                            |
| **Recommended value** | 'current' or 'simple'                                  |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                 |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                          |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                          |

### `push.autoSetupRemote`

Auto-set upstream tracking on first push (no more --set-upstream). Git 2.37+

> **What this does:** Normally, the first time you push a new branch, git makes you type `git push --set-upstream origin my-branch`. With this set to `true`, a simple `git push` just works — git creates the remote branch and sets up tracking automatically.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `true`                      |
| **Current scope**     | global                      |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`             |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'true'                      |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

### `push.followTags`

Push annotated tags along with commits automatically

> **What this does:** Tags are named markers on specific commits (like `v1.0.0`). With this on, `git push` automatically sends any new annotated tags along with your commits, so you don't have to push tags separately.

| Property              | Value                                         |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                     |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                         |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                               |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                   |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' if you use annotated tags for releases |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                        |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                 |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                 |

---

## Merge

*How git handles merges and conflict markers.*

### `merge.ff`

Fast-forward on merge: 'only'=refuse merge commits, 'false'=always create them

> **What this does:** Controls whether `git merge` creates a merge commit or just moves the branch pointer forward. `only` means: if the merge can't be a simple fast-forward, refuse to merge (keeping history linear). `false` means: always create a merge commit, even when fast-forward is possible.

| Property              | Value                                                         |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                     |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                         |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`, `only`                                       |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                   |
| **Recommended value** | 'only' for linear history, 'false' for explicit merge commits |
| **Recommended scope** | local                                                         |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                 |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                 |

### `merge.conflictstyle`

Conflict marker format: 'diff3'/'zdiff3' shows the common ancestor (3-way diff)

> **What this does:** When two people edit the same lines, git inserts conflict markers in the file. The default (`merge`) shows only "their version" vs "your version." With `diff3`/`zdiff3`, you also see the original code before either person changed it, making it much easier to understand what each side intended.

| Property              | Value                                  |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `zdiff3`                               |
| **Current scope**     | global                                 |
| **Valid values**      | `merge`, `diff3`, `zdiff3` (git 2.35+) |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`            |
| **Recommended value** | 'zdiff3' (git 2.35+) or 'diff3'        |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                 |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                          |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                          |

### `merge.tool`

Default tool for resolving merge conflicts interactively

> **What this does:** Instead of manually editing conflict markers in a text file, you can use a visual tool that shows both versions side by side. This setting picks which tool opens when you run `git mergetool`.

| Property              | Value                                                                   |
| :-------------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                               |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                                   |
| **Valid values**      | Any merge tool — e.g. `vscode`, `meld`, `vimdiff`, `kdiff3`, `opendiff` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                             |
| **Recommended value** | e.g. 'vscode', 'meld', 'vimdiff'                                        |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                                  |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                           |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                           |

### `merge.log`

Include one-line descriptions of merged commits in merge commit message

> **What this does:** When git creates a merge commit, this setting adds a summary of all the commits being merged into the merge message — helpful for understanding what a merge brought in.

| Property              | Value                                                |
| :-------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                            |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`, or integer (max entries to include) |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                          |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' for informative merge commits                 |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                               |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                        |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                        |

---

## Rebase

*Rebase workflow helpers: auto-stash, fixup squashing, stacked refs.*

### `rebase.autostash`

Auto-stash uncommitted changes before rebase, unstash after

> **What this does:** If you have uncommitted changes and try to rebase, git normally refuses. With this on, git automatically stashes your changes, does the rebase, then restores them — no manual stash/unstash needed.

| Property              | Value                                      |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `true`                                     |
| **Current scope**     | global                                     |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                            |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' -- prevents 'dirty worktree' errors |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                     |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                              |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                              |

### `rebase.autoSquash`

Auto-apply fixup!/squash! prefixes in interactive rebase (with --fixup)

> **What this does:** When you make a fixup commit (`git commit --fixup <sha>`), this setting automatically moves and squashes it into the right place during interactive rebase. Great for polishing a branch before merging.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `true`                      |
| **Current scope**     | global                      |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`             |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'true'                      |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

### `rebase.updateRefs`

Auto-update stacked branch refs during rebase. Git 2.38+

> **What this does:** If you have multiple branches stacked on top of each other, rebasing the base branch normally leaves the others pointing at the old commits. This setting automatically updates all the stacked branch pointers.

| Property              | Value                               |
| :-------------------- | :---------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                           |
| **Current scope**     | unset                               |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                     |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`         |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' for stacked branch workflows |
| **Recommended scope** | global                              |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                       |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                       |

---

## Rerere

*Reuse recorded resolutions for recurring merge conflicts.*

### `rerere.enabled`

REuse REcorded Resolution -- remember and replay merge conflict fixes

> **What this does:** "Rerere" stands for "reuse recorded resolution." If you resolve a merge conflict, git remembers how you resolved it. The next time the same conflict appears (e.g. during repeated rebases), git applies your previous resolution automatically.

| Property              | Value                                    |
| :-------------------- | :--------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `true`                                   |
| **Current scope**     | global                                   |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                          |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`              |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' -- saves time on repeated rebases |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                   |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                            |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                            |

---

## Stash

*Temporary work storage: stash display and behavior.*

### `stash.showPatch`

Show the patch (diff) in 'git stash show' instead of just a file stat summary

> **What this does:** By default, `git stash show` only lists which files changed. With this on, it shows the actual diff — the line-by-line changes — so you can see exactly what's in each stash without applying it.

| Property              | Value                                |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                            |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                      |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`          |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' for detailed stash inspection |
| **Recommended scope** | global                               |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                        |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                        |

---

## Branch & Init

*Branch/tag display, sorting, and defaults for new repositories.*

### `branch.autosetuprebase`

Auto-set pull.rebase for new tracking branches: 'always', 'local', 'remote', 'never'

> **What this does:** When you create a new branch that tracks a remote branch, this setting automatically configures it to rebase on pull instead of merge. Saves you from setting `pull.rebase` for each branch individually.

| Property              | Value                                        |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                    |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                        |
| **Valid values**      | `always`, `local`, `remote`, `never`         |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                  |
| **Recommended value** | 'always' if you prefer rebasing consistently |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                       |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                |

### `branch.sort`

Default sort for 'git branch': '-committerdate' shows most recent first

> **What this does:** By default, `git branch` lists branches alphabetically. With `-committerdate`, the most recently worked-on branches appear first — much more useful when you have many branches.

| Property              | Value                                                                    |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `-committerdate`                                                         |
| **Current scope**     | global                                                                   |
| **Valid values**      | Sort key — e.g. `-committerdate`, `refname`, `authordate`, `-authordate` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                              |
| **Recommended value** | '-committerdate'                                                         |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                                   |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                            |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                            |

### `init.defaultBranch`

Default branch name for 'git init'

> **What this does:** When you create a brand-new repo with `git init`, this is the name of the first branch. Most projects now use `main` instead of the older default `master`.

| Property              | Value                                                   |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Current value**     | `main`                                                  |
| **Current scope**     | global                                                  |
| **Valid values**      | Branch name — e.g. `main`, `master`, `trunk`, `develop` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                             |
| **Recommended value** | 'main'                                                  |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                  |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                           |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                           |

### `tag.sort`

Sort for 'git tag': 'version:refname' gives natural version ordering (v1.9 < v1.10)

> **What this does:** By default, `git tag` sorts alphabetically, which puts `v1.10` before `v1.9`. Setting this to `version:refname` sorts by version number correctly.

| Property              | Value                                                        |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                    |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                        |
| **Valid values**      | Sort key — e.g. `version:refname`, `refname`, `-creatordate` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                  |
| **Recommended value** | 'version:refname'                                            |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                       |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                |

---

## Identity & Signing

*Author identity and GPG/SSH commit/tag signing.*

### `user.name`

Author name recorded in every commit. Use local scope for work-specific identity

> **What this does:** Your name appears in every commit you make. Set this once globally and override locally if you need a different name for work repos.

| Property              | Value                            |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `Joseph`                         |
| **Current scope**     | global                           |
| **Valid values**      | Your full name — e.g. `Jane Doe` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`      |
| **Recommended value** | Your full name (required)        |
| **Recommended scope** | global                           |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                    |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                    |

### `user.email`

Author email recorded in every commit. Use local scope for per-project email

> **What this does:** Your email appears in every commit. GitHub uses this to link commits to your account. Use a `local` override if your work email differs from your personal one.

| Property              | Value                                        |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `joseph26584@gmail.com`                      |
| **Current scope**     | global                                       |
| **Valid values**      | Your email address — e.g. `jane@example.com` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                  |
| **Recommended value** | Your email (required; local for work repos)  |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                       |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                |

### `commit.gpgsign`

Auto-sign every commit with GPG/SSH key. GitHub shows 'Verified' badge

> **What this does:** Signing commits proves they really came from you. GitHub shows a "Verified" badge next to signed commits. Requires a GPG or SSH key to be set up first.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                       |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`             |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' for verified commits |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

### `tag.gpgsign`

Auto-sign every tag with GPG/SSH key

> **What this does:** Like commit signing, but for tags. Proves a release tag was created by an authorized person.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                       |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`             |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' if signing commits   |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

### `gpg.format`

Signing format: 'openpgp' (GPG) or 'ssh' (SSH keys -- simpler setup)

> **What this does:** Choose between GPG (traditional, more complex setup) or SSH keys (simpler — you may already have one for GitHub authentication). Both produce "Verified" badges on GitHub.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                       |
| **Valid values**      | `openpgp`, `ssh`, `x509`    |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'ssh' for SSH-based signing |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

### `gpg.ssh.allowedSignersFile`

Allowed SSH public keys file for signature verification

> **What this does:** A file listing trusted SSH public keys. Git checks this file to verify signatures locally. Without it, `git log --show-signature` can't confirm who signed a commit.

| Property              | Value                                                                  |
| :-------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                              |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                                  |
| **Valid values**      | Path to an allowed signers file — e.g. `~/.config/git/allowed_signers` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                            |
| **Recommended value** | Set if using SSH signing for local verification                        |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                                 |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                          |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                          |

---

## Commit

*Commit message templates and editor behavior.*

### `commit.template`

Path to default commit message template shown in editor on 'git commit'

> **What this does:** A template file whose contents pre-fill the commit message editor every time you commit. Useful for enforcing a team format (e.g. type, scope, description).

| Property              | Value                                                           |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `.gitmessage.txt`                                               |
| **Current scope**     | local                                                           |
| **Valid values**      | Path to template file — e.g. `.gitmessage.txt`, `~/.gitmessage` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                     |
| **Recommended value** | '.gitmessage.txt' for this project                              |
| **Recommended scope** | local                                                           |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                   |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                   |

### `commit.verbose`

Show diff of staged changes in commit message editor for review

> **What this does:** When writing a commit message, this shows the full diff of your staged changes right in the editor. Helpful for reviewing exactly what you're about to commit.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                       |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`             |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'true'                      |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

---

## Diff

*Diff algorithm, moved-line detection, and rename handling.*

### `diff.algorithm`

Diff algorithm: 'histogram' gives better results than default 'myers'

> **What this does:** Git uses an algorithm to figure out what changed between two versions of a file. `histogram` generally produces more readable diffs than the default `myers`, especially for moved code blocks.

| Property              | Value                                                 |
| :-------------------- | :---------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `histogram`                                           |
| **Current scope**     | global                                                |
| **Valid values**      | `myers` (default), `minimal`, `patience`, `histogram` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                           |
| **Recommended value** | 'histogram'                                           |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                         |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                         |

### `diff.colorMoved`

Highlight moved lines in diffs (not just added/deleted). Makes refactoring clearer

> **What this does:** When you move code from one place to another, normal diffs show it as deleted + added. This setting highlights moved lines in a different color so you can tell they were moved, not rewritten.

| Property              | Value                                                       |
| :-------------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                       |
| **Valid values**      | `no`, `default`, `plain`, `blocks`, `zebra`, `dimmed-zebra` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                 |
| **Recommended value** | 'default' or 'zebra'                                        |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                               |

### `diff.colorMovedWS`

Whitespace handling for moved-line detection

> **What this does:** Controls whether whitespace changes (like re-indenting) prevent moved-line detection. `allow-indentation-change` means moving code into a nested block still counts as "moved."

| Property              | Value                                                                                              |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                                                          |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                                                              |
| **Valid values**      | `no`, `allow-indentation-change`, `ignore-space-at-eol`, `ignore-space-change`, `ignore-all-space` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                                                        |
| **Recommended value** | 'allow-indentation-change' with colorMoved                                                         |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                                                             |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                                                      |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                                                      |

### `diff.tool`

Default tool for side-by-side diff comparison

> **What this does:** When you run `git difftool`, this picks which visual diff program opens. VS Code, Meld, and delta are popular choices.

| Property              | Value                                                               |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                           |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                               |
| **Valid values**      | Any diff tool — e.g. `vscode`, `meld`, `delta`, `vimdiff`, `kdiff3` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                         |
| **Recommended value** | e.g. 'vscode', 'meld', 'delta'                                      |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                              |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                       |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                       |

### `diff.renameLimit`

Max files for rename detection. Increase if git misses renames in large changesets

> **What this does:** Git tries to detect when a file was renamed rather than deleted and re-created. In large changesets, rename detection can be slow or miss renames. Increasing this limit makes it try harder.

| Property              | Value                                          |
| :-------------------- | :--------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                      |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                          |
| **Valid values**      | Integer — e.g. `0` (disabled), `5000`, `10000` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                    |
| **Recommended value** | Increase (e.g. 5000) for large repos           |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                         |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                  |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                  |

### `diff.submodule`

Submodule diff format: 'log' shows commit summaries, 'short' shows only SHAs

> **What this does:** Submodules are repos inside repos. This controls how their changes show in diffs — `log` shows the commit messages, which is more informative than just SHAs.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                       |
| **Valid values**      | `short`, `log`, `diff`      |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'log' if you use submodules |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

---

## Log & Display

*Date format, color output, and column display for listings.*

### `log.date`

Default date format for 'git log': 'relative', 'short', 'iso', 'local'

> **What this does:** Controls how dates appear in `git log`. `relative` shows "3 days ago", `short` shows "2026-03-16", `iso` shows full timestamps.

| Property              | Value                                                                  |
| :-------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                              |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                                  |
| **Valid values**      | `relative`, `short`, `local`, `iso`, `iso-strict`, `rfc`, `format:...` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                            |
| **Recommended value** | 'relative' or 'short'                                                  |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                                 |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                          |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                          |

### `color.ui`

Enable colored output: 'auto' enables when stdout is a terminal

> **What this does:** Enables colored output in the terminal (green for added lines, red for removed, etc.). `auto` turns color on when outputting to a terminal but off when piping to a file.

| Property              | Value                          |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                      |
| **Current scope**     | unset                          |
| **Valid values**      | `auto`, `always`, `false`      |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`    |
| **Recommended value** | 'auto' (default in modern git) |
| **Recommended scope** | global                         |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                  |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                  |

### `column.ui`

Display branch/tag listings in columns when terminal is wide enough

> **What this does:** When listing branches or tags, `auto` arranges them in columns (like `ls` does for files) to fit more on screen.

| Property              | Value                                               |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                           |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                               |
| **Valid values**      | `auto`, `always`, `never`, `column`, `row`, `plain` |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                         |
| **Recommended value** | 'auto'                                              |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                              |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                       |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                       |

---

## Credential & Transfer

*Credential storage, SSL, and object integrity verification.*

### `credential.helper`

How git stores credentials (passwords/tokens). 'manager' uses OS credential store

> **What this does:** Instead of typing your password or token every time you push/pull, a credential helper stores it securely. `manager` uses your OS keychain (Windows Credential Manager or macOS Keychain).

| Property              | Value                                                                                      |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `manager`                                                                                  |
| **Current scope**     | system                                                                                     |
| **Valid values**      | `manager` (Windows/macOS), `osxkeychain` (macOS), `store` (plaintext), `cache` (in-memory) |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                                                |
| **Recommended value** | 'manager' (Windows), 'osxkeychain' (macOS), 'store' (Linux)                                |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                                                     |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                                              |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                                              |

### `http.sslVerify`

Verify SSL certs for HTTPS. Disabling is a security risk

> **What this does:** Git verifies HTTPS certificates to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks. Disabling this is a security risk — only do it for self-signed certificates in trusted environments.

| Property              | Value                                                     |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                 |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                     |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                                           |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                               |
| **Recommended value** | Keep 'true' (default); only disable for self-signed certs |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                    |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                             |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                             |

### `transfer.fsckobjects`

Verify object integrity during push/pull transfers

> **What this does:** Runs integrity checks on data during push and pull. Catches corruption that could silently break your repository. Worth enabling for important projects.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                       |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`             |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' for data integrity   |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

---

## Performance

*Garbage collection, index format, and large-repo optimizations.*

### `gc.auto`

Threshold for auto garbage collection (loose object count)

> **What this does:** Git stores objects as loose files. Over time, these accumulate and slow things down. `gc.auto` triggers automatic cleanup when the count exceeds the threshold.

| Property              | Value                                                                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                                       |
| **Valid values**      | Integer (loose object count) — e.g. `6700` (default), `0` (disable auto-gc) |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                                 |
| **Recommended value** | Default (6700) is fine; increase for busy repos                             |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                               |

### `feature.manyFiles`

Enable optimizations for repos with many files (untracked cache, fsmonitor)

> **What this does:** Enables performance optimizations for repos with thousands of files — things like the untracked file cache and filesystem monitor. Not needed for small-to-medium repos.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                       |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`             |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' for large repos      |
| **Recommended scope** | local                       |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

### `index.version`

Index format version. V4 is more compact and faster

> **What this does:** The index (staging area) file format. Version 4 is more compact and faster, especially in repos with many files.

| Property              | Value                                                         |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                     |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                         |
| **Valid values**      | `2` (legacy), `3`, `4` (compact, recommended for large repos) |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                   |
| **Recommended value** | '4' for large repos                                           |
| **Recommended scope** | local                                                         |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                 |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                 |

---

## Help & UX

*Autocorrect, advice messages, and other UX settings.*

### `help.autocorrect`

Auto-correct mistyped commands: 'prompt'=ask, N=wait N deciseconds, '0'=off

> **What this does:** If you mistype a command (e.g. `git statuus` instead of `git status`), git can auto-correct it. `prompt` asks first; a number auto-runs after that many tenths of a second.

| Property              | Value                                                                           |
| :-------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                                       |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                                           |
| **Valid values**      | `0` (off), `prompt` (ask), or integer (decisecond delay) — e.g. `10` = 1 second |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                                     |
| **Recommended value** | 'prompt' or '10' (1 second delay)                                               |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                                          |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                                   |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                                   |

### `status.showUntrackedFiles`

Untracked file display: 'all'=individual files, 'normal'=group by directory

> **What this does:** `all` shows every untracked file individually. `normal` groups them by directory. `all` is more verbose but ensures you never miss a file that should be committed or gitignored.

| Property              | Value                         |
| :-------------------- | :---------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                     |
| **Current scope**     | unset                         |
| **Valid values**      | `all`, `normal`, `no`         |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`   |
| **Recommended value** | 'all' for complete visibility |
| **Recommended scope** | global                        |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                 |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                 |

### `advice.detachedHead`

Show the advice message when entering detached HEAD state

> **What this does:** "Detached HEAD" means you're not on a branch — you checked out a specific commit or tag. Git shows a long warning about this. If you do it often (e.g. reviewing old releases), set to `false` to silence it.

| Property              | Value                                                        |
| :-------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                    |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                        |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                                              |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                  |
| **Recommended value** | 'false' to suppress if you frequently check out tags or SHAs |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                       |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                                |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                                |

### `advice.statusHints`

Show hints in 'git status' about staging, unstaging, and conflict resolution

> **What this does:** `git status` normally includes hints like "use git add to stage" or "use git restore to discard changes." Helpful when learning git; set to `false` once you know the commands.

| Property              | Value                                                      |
| :-------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                                                  |
| **Current scope**     | unset                                                      |
| **Valid values**      | `true`, `false`                                            |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system`                                |
| **Recommended value** | 'true' (default) for beginners, 'false' for cleaner output |
| **Recommended scope** | global                                                     |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_                                              |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_                                              |

---

## Protocol

*Git wire protocol version for network communication.*

### `protocol.version`

Git protocol version: v2 is faster (partial clone, faster negotiation). Git 2.26+

> **What this does:** Git protocol v2 is more efficient — faster clones, smarter negotiation of what data to transfer. It's the default in modern git versions.

| Property              | Value                       |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------- |
| **Current value**     | `(unset)`                   |
| **Current scope**     | unset                       |
| **Valid values**      | `0`, `1`, `2`               |
| **Valid scopes**      | `local`, `global`, `system` |
| **Recommended value** | '2' (default in modern git) |
| **Recommended scope** | global                      |
| **Desired value**     | _(no change)_               |
| **Desired scope**     | _(no change)_               |

---

## Glossary

Quick reference for git configuration terminology used in this file.

| Term                  | Meaning                                                                                                                                                   |
| :-------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **scope**             | Where a config value is stored: `local` (this repo), `global` (all your repos), or `system` (all users). Local overrides global, global overrides system. |
| **local**             | Config stored in `.git/config` — applies to this repository only. Best for project-specific settings (e.g. commit template, email for work repos).        |
| **global**            | Config stored in `~/.gitconfig` — applies to all your repositories. Best for personal preferences (e.g. editor, signing, aliases).                        |
| **system**            | Config stored in `/etc/gitconfig` (Linux/macOS) or `C:\\Program Files\\Git\\etc\\gitconfig` (Windows). Requires admin access. Rarely needed.              |
| **unset**             | The key has no value at any scope. Git uses its built-in default.                                                                                         |
| **override**          | When the same key is set at multiple scopes, the most specific scope wins: local > global > system.                                                       |
| **(no change)**       | Placeholder in the Desired columns meaning "leave as-is." Replace with a value to apply a change.                                                         |
| **recommended**       | The opinionated default suggested by this catalog. Not mandatory — adjust to your workflow.                                                               |
| **LF / CRLF**         | Line ending styles: LF (`\\n`, Linux/macOS) vs CRLF (`\\r\\n`, Windows). See `core.autocrlf` and `core.eol`.                                              |
| **fast-forward**      | A merge where the branch pointer simply moves forward (no merge commit). Controlled by `pull.ff` and `merge.ff`.                                          |
| **rebase**            | Replaying commits on top of another branch instead of merging. See `pull.rebase`, `rebase.autostash`.                                                     |
| **rerere**            | REuse REcorded Resolution — git remembers how you resolved a conflict and auto-applies it next time. See `rerere.enabled`.                                |
| **GPG / SSH signing** | Cryptographic signatures on commits/tags. GitHub shows a "Verified" badge. See `commit.gpgsign`, `gpg.format`.                                            |
| **delta**             | A popular terminal diff viewer with syntax highlighting. Used as `core.pager` or `diff.tool`. See <https://github.com/dandavison/delta>.                  |
| **fsck**              | File System Check — integrity verification of git objects. See `fetch.fsckobjects`, `transfer.fsckobjects`.                                               |
| **refspec**           | A mapping between remote and local refs (e.g. `+refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*`). Controls what `fetch` and `push` transfer.                          |

---

## Customizing This Reference

This reference is generated from the `GIT_CONFIG_CATALOG` list in
`scripts/git_doctor.py`. To add or remove config entries:

### Adding a New Config Entry

1. Add a tuple to `GIT_CONFIG_CATALOG` in the appropriate section:

   ```python
   ("section.key", "recommended_scope", "Description of what it does", "recommended value"),
   ```

2. Add a valid-values entry to `CONFIG_VALID_VALUES`:

   ```python
   "section.key": "`value1`, `value2`, `value3`",
   ```

3. If the key belongs to a new section, add it to `CONFIG_SECTION_MAP`
   and `SECTION_DESCRIPTIONS`.

4. Regenerate this file:

   ```bash
   python scripts/git_doctor.py --export-config
   ```

### Removing a Config Entry

1. Delete the tuple from `GIT_CONFIG_CATALOG`.
2. Delete the key from `CONFIG_VALID_VALUES` (if present).
3. Regenerate this file.

### Notes

- The `--apply-recommended` flag iterates `GIT_CONFIG_CATALOG`.
  Adding or removing entries there changes what gets applied.
- Entries with recommendation text that starts with common skip
  patterns (e.g. "e.g.", "Leave unset", "Your", "Set if",
  "Auto-detected", "Default is", "Keep", "Increase") are skipped
  by `--apply-recommended` because they lack a concrete default.
- Git occasionally adds or deprecates config keys across versions.
  Run `git config --list --show-origin` to see what your version supports.

### Implementation Note

`GIT_CONFIG_CATALOG` is a list of 4-tuples and `CONFIG_VALID_VALUES`
is a separate dict. If a key is in the catalog but not in the dict,
the export shows _(freeform)_. Currently all 62 keys are covered —
keep them in sync when adding new ones.

If you add new tables to the export in the future, use
`_aligned_md_table()` to keep the alignment consistent.

---

## .gitattributes Reference

`.gitattributes` controls per-path settings in your repository.
Unlike `.gitconfig`, these settings are **committed and shared**
with all collaborators. Place this file in the repository root.

### Key Terms

| Term             | Meaning                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| :--------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **eol**          | End Of Line — the character(s) that mark the end of a line in a text file. Controlled per-path in `.gitattributes` via `eol=lf` or `eol=crlf`.                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| **LF**           | Line Feed (`\n`, hex `0x0A`). The standard line ending on Linux and macOS. One byte per line break. Used as the canonical format inside git's object store.                                                                                                                                                                      |
| **CRLF**         | Carriage Return + Line Feed (`\r\n`, hex `0x0D 0x0A`). The standard line ending on Windows. Two bytes per line break. Git can auto-convert CRLF↔LF via `core.autocrlf` or `.gitattributes` rules.                                                                                                                                |
| **diff drivers** | Built-in or custom programs that control how `git diff` displays changes for specific file types. For example, `diff=python` tells git to use Python-aware function boundary detection, so diff headers show `def func_name()` instead of generic context. Common built-in drivers: `python`, `markdown`, `html`, `css`, `java`. |

### Sample .gitattributes

```text
# ── Line Endings ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Force LF in the repo, let git handle working-tree conversion
* text=auto eol=lf

# Explicitly mark text files (override auto-detection)
*.py    text eol=lf
*.md    text eol=lf
*.yml   text eol=lf
*.yaml  text eol=lf
*.toml  text eol=lf
*.json  text eol=lf
*.html  text eol=lf
*.css   text eol=lf
*.js    text eol=lf
*.sh    text eol=lf

# Windows-specific files that need CRLF
*.bat   text eol=crlf
*.cmd   text eol=crlf
*.ps1   text eol=crlf

# ── Binary Files ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Mark binary files so git doesn't try to diff/merge them
*.png    binary
*.jpg    binary
*.jpeg   binary
*.gif    binary
*.ico    binary
*.woff   binary
*.woff2  binary
*.ttf    binary
*.zip    binary
*.tar.gz binary
*.sqlite3 binary
*.db     binary
*.pdf    binary

# ── Diff Drivers ─────────────────────────────────────────
# Tell git to use language-aware diff heuristics for these file types.
# This improves diff output by showing function/section names in headers.
*.md diff=markdown
*.py diff=python

# ── Export Ignore ────────────────────────────────────────
# Files excluded from 'git archive' exports
.gitattributes   export-ignore
.gitignore       export-ignore
.github/         export-ignore
tests/           export-ignore
docs/            export-ignore
Taskfile.yml     export-ignore
*.code-workspace export-ignore

# ── Linguist (GitHub) ────────────────────────────────────
# Override GitHub's language statistics
docs/*    linguist-documentation
scripts/* linguist-vendored=false
```

### Pattern Syntax

| Pattern                  | Meaning                                                                                  |
| :----------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `*`                      | Match all files                                                                          |
| `*.py`                   | Match files ending in .py                                                                |
| `text`                   | Treat as text (enable line-ending conversion)                                            |
| `text=auto`              | Let git auto-detect text vs binary; convert line endings for detected text files         |
| `binary`                 | Treat as binary — skip newline conversion and textual diff entirely                      |
| `eol=lf`                 | Normalize line endings to LF (`\n`) when checking in and checking out                    |
| `eol=crlf`               | Convert to CRLF (`\r\n`) in the working tree while storing as LF in the repo             |
| `diff=python`            | Use Python-aware diff heuristics (function names in diff headers)                        |
| `diff=markdown`          | Use Markdown-aware diff (section headings in diff headers)                               |
| `export-ignore`          | Exclude from `git archive` exports (tarballs, zips)                                      |
| `linguist-documentation` | Tell GitHub to classify these files as documentation (not source code) in language stats |

---

## Applying Changes from This File

Edit **both** the **Desired value** and **Desired scope** columns
for any config entry above, then apply all changes at once:

```bash
python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-from git-config-reference.md
```

**How it works:**

- Only entries where **both** `Desired value` **and** `Desired scope`
  are changed from `_(no change)_` are applied.
- If you set a **Desired value** but leave **Desired scope** unchanged
  (or vice versa), the entry is **skipped with an error**.
- Complete entries in other sections are still applied normally.

To apply the catalog's **recommended** values for **all** keys
(overwriting existing values):

```bash
python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-recommended              # apply all
python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-recommended --dry-run   # preview first
```

> **Warning:** `--apply-recommended` overwrites existing values
> to match the catalog baseline. For per-key control, edit this
> file and use `--apply-from` instead.

To apply only the **core minimal set** (12 high-impact keys):

```bash
python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-recommended-minimal              # apply core set
python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-recommended-minimal --dry-run   # preview first
```

To preview what would change without applying:

```bash
python scripts/git_doctor.py --apply-from git-config-reference.md --dry-run
```
